children.value: NWB Files have a section for providing the visual content used as stimulus during a session. These can be in the form of images or videos, and are stored in the file’s
section. This notebook shows how to retrieve and display this information from multiple NWB files with different NWB storage formats.
Note: If running on a new environment, run this cell once and then restart the kernel

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import io
import ipywidgets as widgets
For displaying image templates, we will use the public dandiset
Differential encoding of temporal context and expectation
. This dataset contains both natural and artificial stimulus in the form of images. The
section takes the form of a dictionary of template groups. Each template group is also a dictionary which contains images. Below, you can change
to access different files if you wish. The file will be downloaded and opened.

# This can sometimes take a while depending on the size of the file
nwb_io1 = dandi_download_open(dandiset_id, dandi_filepath, download_loc)
nwb1 = nwb_io1.read()

children.value: You can select an individual stimulus template image by using the keys printed. The keys to select a template group are printed above. Once a template group is selected, you can pick a key from the template group to select and individual template image. In this example, a natural image is selected and displayed.
It is common for visual stimulus to also include artificial stimulus images. One such image is selected and shown below.

Here, all the stimulus images are retrieved from the file and put into an list of images. A subset of these are displayed together. If your NWB file stores template information differently, this may not work. In this particular example, the first 100 image from the session are displayed.

all_stim_templates = []
for template_set in nwb1.stimulus_template.values():
    all_stim_templates += list(template_set.images.values())

print(len(all_stim_templates))
# there are > 2000 templates; only show the first 100; change this to suit your needs
stim_templates = all_stim_templates[:100]

%matplotlib inline

n_cols = 5
n_rows = len(stim_templates) // n_cols
fig, axes = plt.subplots(n_rows+1, n_cols)
fig.set_size_inches(3*n_cols, 2*n_rows) # can tweak these if sizing/spacing needs improvement

if len(axes.shape) == 1:
    axes = axes.reshape((1, axes.shape[0]))

for i, template in enumerate(stim_templates):
    ax_row = int(i / n_cols)
    ax_col = i % n_cols
    axes[ax_row][ax_col].imshow(template, cmap="gray")

for ax in axes.flat:
    ax.axis("off")

section can also be used to store stimulus movies. For this dataset, the movie is stored as a template group within the stimulus templates dictionary. You can change the key used to below to select a different move. The movie can be accessed with the
method to display and play this movie below.

# This can sometimes take a while depending on the size of the file
nwb_io2 = dandi_download_open(dandiset_id, dandi_filepath, download_loc)
nwb2 = nwb_io2.read()

children.value:
def hyperslicer(data, play_buttons=False, **kwargs):
    img_widget = widgets.Image(format='png')
    slider = widgets.IntSlider(min=0, max=len(data)-1, value=0, description='Frame')

    def render_frame(frame):
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.tick_params(left=False, bottom=False, labelleft=False, labelbottom=False)
        ax.imshow(data[frame], **kwargs)
        buf = io.BytesIO()
        fig.savefig(buf, format='png', bbox_inches='tight')
        plt.close(fig)
        buf.seek(0)
        img_widget.value = buf.read()

    render_frame(0)
    slider.observe(lambda change: render_frame(change['new']), names='value')
    display(widgets.VBox([img_widget, slider]))
